event: Austronesian voyagers from island Southeast Asia begin settling the western Caroline Islands and Marianas, marking the earliest human habitation of Micronesia.
Settlers reach the central Caroline Islands including the high islands of Chuuk, Pohnpei, and Kosrae, establishing distinct island societies.
Atoll communities develop across the eastern Carolines and Marshall Islands, mastering long-distance canoe navigation using stars, swells, and stick charts.
Stratified chiefdoms and complex social hierarchies emerge on the high islands, supported by breadfruit, taro, and fishing economies.
Construction begins on Nan Madol, a vast complex of basalt and coral islets off Pohnpei that becomes the ceremonial seat of the Saudeleur dynasty.
The Saudeleur dynasty consolidates centralized rule over Pohnpei from Nan Madol, an unprecedented political unification in the region.
The Lelu stone city is built on Kosrae as the residence of the island's paramount chiefs, rivaling Nan Madol in monumental architecture.
The Saudeleur dynasty falls and is replaced by the decentralized Nahnmwarki chiefdom system that still structures Pohnpeian society.
Ferdinand Magellan's expedition reaches Guam and the Marianas, the first European contact with Micronesia.
Portuguese and Spanish navigators sight and chart islands in the Caroline group during voyages across the Pacific.
Spain formally claims the Mariana and Caroline Islands as part of its Pacific empire administered from the Philippines.
Spanish Jesuit missionaries led by Diego Luis de San Vitores begin evangelizing the Marianas, initiating sustained European presence.
Spanish explorer Francisco Lazcano charts and names the Caroline Islands in honor of King Charles II of Spain.
Increasing visits by Western whalers, traders, and beachcombers introduce new goods and devastating epidemic diseases to the islands.
American Protestant missionaries of the ABCFM establish a mission on Kosrae and later Pohnpei, transforming island religion and society.
Germany and Spain dispute control of the Carolines; Pope Leo XIII mediates, affirming Spanish sovereignty while granting Germany trading rights.
Spain sells the Caroline, Mariana (except Guam), and Palau islands to Germany after losing the Spanish-American War.
Pohnpeians of the Sokehs district rise against harsh German labor policies in the Sokehs Rebellion, which is brutally suppressed in 1911.
Japan seizes Germany's Micronesian colonies at the outbreak of World War I and occupies the islands.
The League of Nations grants Japan a South Seas Mandate over Micronesia, beginning intensive Japanese settlement and economic development.
Japanese colonists outnumber islanders in parts of Micronesia as sugar, fishing, and phosphate industries and military bases expand.
U.S. forces strike the Japanese naval base at Chuuk Lagoon in Operation Hailstone and capture key islands during the Pacific campaign.
The United Nations establishes the Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands under U.S. administration, covering the Carolines, Marshalls, and Marianas.
The U.S. moves the Trust Territory capital to Saipan and expands civilian administration and education across the islands.
The Congress of Micronesia convenes, giving the islands a unified legislative body and a forum to debate future political status.
Voters in four districts approve a constitution to form the Federated States of Micronesia, while the Marshalls, Palau, and Marianas choose separate paths.
The Constitution of the Federated States of Micronesia takes effect, uniting Yap, Chuuk, Pohnpei, and Kosrae with Tosiwo Nakayama as first president.
The Federated States of Micronesia signs the Compact of Free Association with the United States, defining their future relationship.
The Compact of Free Association enters into force, granting the FSM independence in free association with the United States.
The UN Security Council formally terminates the trusteeship over the Federated States of Micronesia.
The Federated States of Micronesia is admitted as a full member of the United Nations.
The FSM joins the South Pacific Forum and deepens regional cooperation on fisheries, trade, and environmental issues.
The amended Compact of Free Association with the United States takes effect, restructuring economic assistance and creating a trust fund.
Severe king tides and storm surges inundate low-lying atolls, highlighting the nation's acute vulnerability to sea-level rise.
Pohnpei's Nan Madol gains greater international attention as efforts begin to secure UNESCO World Heritage recognition.
Typhoon Maysak devastates Chuuk and Yap states, destroying homes, crops, and infrastructure on outer islands.
Nan Madol is inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site and simultaneously listed as endangered due to siltation and decay.
Chuuk State debates a referendum on seceding from the Federated States of Micronesia, which is repeatedly postponed.
President David Panuelo takes office and pursues a more assertive foreign policy amid growing U.S.-China competition in the Pacific.
The FSM closes its borders early in the COVID-19 pandemic, keeping the country among the last places on Earth free of community transmission.
The FSM signs a renewed 20-year Compact of Free Association agreement with the United States securing long-term economic assistance.
The U.S. Congress funds the renewed Compact, providing billions in assistance through 2043 amid strategic competition with China.
